opp.name: KPJ Healthcare_E-procurement
opp.next_step: Confidence 40% 1st June Regular catch ups with Dr Siva. - interested to attend Horizon and keen for options to replace Ariba Working to meet Dr. Siva (Board Member) along with Chin Keat 16th Mar - GJ working on setting Meeting with CEO (Chin Keat) 12th Mar CPO meeting being setup after Raya - April Meting on 25th with proc and IT teams - unhappy with the way Ariba is rolled out. Wants to replicate IJN story - Next meeting with CPO in the planning
opp.ais_why:
• Across 332 chars of NextStep covering five dated entries from Mar to May 2026 ‚Äî including CEO meeting setup, CPO Raya meeting, and a reference customer story ‚Äî zero mention of AI, Merlin, agentic, automation or any AI capability. Product scope contains no AI SKUs (iContract; iSource; eProcurement; eInvoicing; iSaaS). On 12 Mar 2026: 'CPO meeting being setup after Raya - April Meting on 25th with proc and IT teams - unhappy with the way Ariba is rolled out. Wants to replicate IJN story.'
• The 30% confidence Mar update: 'Working to meet Dr. Siva (Board Member) along with Chin Keat.' KPJ wants to replicate the IJN story (Institut Jantung Negara, a Malaysian hospital reference) ‚Äî a deployment-pattern reference, not an AI capability reference. The deal is anchored in Ariba dissatisfaction and replication of a peer hospital, with no AI-specific evaluation criterion captured in any of the five rep notes.
• Confidence is Medium given the NextStep is short. With no AI products in scope and no AI keyword across the entire history, score 2 (AI Resistant) reflects a traditional e-procurement displacement deal where the buyer pattern is 'do what IJN did' rather than 'embrace AI'.

sources: Next_Step__c field, dated 1 Jun 2026
Avoma: 4 meeting attempts (Aug 2025, Feb 2026), all cancelled/not recorded, zero transcribed content
metrics.narrative: No quantified business case on record. Next_Step__c (1 Jun 2026) states the buyer is 'unhappy with the way Ariba is rolled out' and 'wants to replicate IJN story,' indicating qualitative dissatisfaction with the incumbent and a reference-customer benchmark (IJN), but no cost savings, cycle-time reduction, error-rate improvement, or ROI figure has been articulated or logged. Zero transcribed buyer calls means no validated pain quantification. The deal has been in Qualified for 121 days without a validated value case, which blocks credible advancement to the relevant stakeholder or Shortlisted. Next move: secure a discovery call with the Head of Category Management (Mohd hafeiz Idrus, Decision Maker contact role) and the procurement/IT leads mentioned in the 25 Feb meeting attempt to quantify the Ariba pain (cost, manual workarounds, compliance gaps, cycle time) and translate 'replicate IJN' into a具体 savings/efficiency target anchored to KPJ's procurement spend and volumes.

sources: OpportunityContactRole: no Champion role; Decision Maker and Evaluator roles present but no advocacy evidence
Next_Step__c 1 Jun: mentions board member engagement (unconfirmed identity/advocacy)
Zero transcribed calls to validate champion behavior
champion.narrative: No champion identified or validated. Zero transcribed buyer calls and no contact role of Champion means we have no confirmed internal advocate navigating this deal. Next_Step__c (1 Jun 2026) mentions 'regular catch ups' with a board member interested in Horizon and Ariba replacement, and work to meet this board member 'along with the relevant stakeholder' (CEO), suggesting the board member may be a senior sponsor, but their identity, role, and level of advocacy are unconfirmed (they are not in the contact roles, and the name is not verifiable against the account contact list or Avoma attendees). The Decision Maker contact role (Mohd hafeiz Idrus, Head of Category Management) is a functional buyer but shows no evidence of champion behavior (no introductions, no internal selling, no competitive intelligence shared). The Evaluator contact role (Amir Farid, Deputy Manager Procurement) is even more junior and passive. After 121 days in Qualified with no champion, no multi-threading, and no recorded buyer engagement, we are flying blind—no one is pulling us through the organization, and we have no internal read on the competitive landscape, decision timeline, or political dynamics. This is a high-risk single-thread deal. Next move: use the next engagement (discovery call or CEO meeting) to identify and cultivate a champion—someone with access to the Economic Buyer, a vested interest in solving the Ariba pain, and willingness to advocate for Zycus internally. Prioritize the Head of Category Management (Mohd hafeiz) or a senior procurement/transformation lead (e.g. Azmi Abdullah, Head TMO, if involved) and test their advocacy by asking them to facilitate introductions to the CPO and CEO, share the internal evaluation timeline, and provide competitive intelligence (are other vendors being considered?).

sources: Next_Step__c 1 Jun 2026: 'replace Ariba', 'unhappy with the way Ariba is rolled out'
No other competitors named in Salesforce or Avoma
No transcribed competitive intelligence from buyer
competition.narrative: SAP Ariba is the incumbent being displaced, confirmed from Next_Step__c (1 Jun 2026: 'keen for options to replace Ariba', 'unhappy with the way Ariba is rolled out'). Ariba's status is high-threat incumbent with active dissatisfaction, but no evidence of formal elimination or Ariba counter-offer. The pain (poor rollout, dissatisfaction) creates an opening, but without a quantified cost comparison, a champion to drive the switch, or an Economic Buyer to authorize the displacement, Ariba remains entrenched by default (inertia and switching cost are real competitive forces). No other external competitors (Coupa, GEP, Jaggaer, SAP Fieldglass, local/regional procurement platforms) are mentioned, but the absence may reflect incomplete discovery rather than sole-sourced status—zero transcribed calls means we have not asked 'who else are you looking at?' The Next_Step mentions 'replicate IJN story,' suggesting KPJ may be benchmarking peer healthcare organizations' vendor choices, but whether IJN uses Zycus or another platform is unstated. The 'do nothing' / status quo (continue with Ariba despite dissatisfaction, or defer the replacement) is a real alternative given the deal's 121-day stall in Qualified. Our wedge: KPJ wants a healthcare-specific procurement solution that avoids Ariba's deployment and usability pain, with a proven reference case (IJN). How we win: position Zycus as the healthcare-proven Ariba alternative with a smoother implementation, local support in APAC, and a具体 ROI case tied to the pain we validate, and bring the IJN story to life (customer call, case study) to de-risk the switch. Activation: secure the discovery call to quantify Ariba's pain, run a healthcare-tailored demo contrasting Zycus vs Ariba on the pain points, and facilitate an IJN reference customer conversation before the CPO/CEO decision meeting.

sources: Next_Step__c 1 Jun 2026: 'unhappy with the way Ariba is rolled out', 'wants to replicate IJN story'
Avoma meeting 25 Feb 2026 (not recorded) noted as surfacing Ariba dissatisfaction
No transcribed call with quantified pain
identify_pain.narrative: Pain partially identified from Next_Step__c but not quantified or validated on a call. The rep log (1 Jun 2026) states KPJ is 'unhappy with the way Ariba is rolled out,' indicating dissatisfaction with the incumbent's deployment (likely poor user adoption, implementation complexity, or lack of local support/customization). The desire to 'replicate IJN story' suggests KPJ sees a peer healthcare success case (IJN, likely Institut Jantung Negara, a Malaysian cardiac center) as a proof point, implying they want a solution that has worked in a similar healthcare procurement context. The meeting on 25 Feb with 'proc and IT teams' was noted as surfacing unhappiness with Ariba, but no recording exists to capture the specific pain points (cost overruns, manual workarounds, poor integration, compliance gaps, vendor unresponsiveness). Pain owner is likely Mohd hafeiz Idrus (Head of Category Management, Decision Maker contact role) and/or the CPO (not yet mapped). Confirmed pain: Ariba rollout dissatisfaction. Inferred pain (not validated): high TCO, implementation complexity, lack of fit for KPJ's multi-hospital procurement model. No quantified impact (cost, time, risk). Next move: run a pain-validation discovery call with Mohd hafeiz and the procurement/IT stakeholders to articulate the Ariba pain in具体 terms—what is broken (process, cost, compliance, user experience), what is the annual cost/impact, who is affected (procurement team, hospital units, finance), and what would success look like (the 'IJN story' translated into KPJ's具体 outcomes).

sources: Zero evidence in Salesforce or Avoma of contracting, legal, InfoSec, or integration discussion
No Current_Contract_Expiration__c populated
Ground truth note suggests partner-led, but partner contracting role unconfirmed
paper_process.narrative: Paper process (contracting, legal, InfoSec, procurement mechanics) completely unknown. Zero transcribed calls, no MSA/SOW discussion logged, no Current_Contract_Expiration__c date populated (so no forcing function from incumbent Ariba contract end-date), and no mention of legal, InfoSec, or ERP integration requirements in Next_Step__c or tasks. KPJ is a large healthcare group, so InfoSec review (patient-data handling, PDPA compliance in Malaysia, system-access controls) and integration with existing ERP/finance systems are likely mandatory gates, but none are surfaced. The deal is in Qualified (121 days) with no commercial or contracting conversation logged, so paper process has not been broached. If this is partner-led (as ground truth note suggests), the partner may be managing contracting, but that is unconfirmed. Next move: in the next buyer engagement (discovery call or CPO meeting), ask explicitly about contracting requirements—does KPJ use a standard MSA, what is the legal review process and typical duration, what InfoSec/compliance documentation is required (questionnaire, pen-test reports, data-residency commitments), who handles procurement execution (central procurement vs hospital-unit procurement), and whether an SI or implementation partner is required or preferred for rollout across KPJ's hospital network. Capture this in writing so the deal plan can account for legal/InfoSec lead time (often 4–8 weeks in healthcare).

sources: OpportunityContactRole query: no EB or Executive Sponsor role

Account contact list includes CEO Keat Chin, not yet tied to this opp
economic_buyer.narrative: Economic Buyer not identified. No contact role of Economic Buyer or Executive Sponsor is present. Next_Step__c (1 Jun 2026) mentions work to meet 'CEO (Chin Keat)' (likely Keat Chin or Keat Chyuan Chin, President & Managing Director per account contacts) and references a 'board member' engaged in catch-ups, but neither has been formally mapped as EB or added to the opportunity contact roles. The Decision Maker contact role is Mohd hafeiz Idrus (Head of Category Management), a procurement-function lead who is unlikely to control a $250k multi-product SaaS budget without C-level or CFO sign-off in a healthcare group of KPJ's scale. Zero transcribed calls means no validation of who approves this budget, whether procurement has delegated authority, or what the approval chain looks like. After 121 days in Qualified with no EB mapped, this is a critical gap that will block any commercial or pricing conversation. Next move: use the planned CEO and board-member engagements to confirm who holds budget authority for a procurement platform investment of this size, and add that person (likely CEO or CFO) as Economic Buyer contact role.

sources: Next_Step__c 1 Jun 2026: mentions CPO meeting, CEO engagement, board member, proc+IT team involvement
OpportunityContactRole: Decision Maker = Mohd hafeiz (Head of Category Management), implies procurement-led process
No transcribed calls to validate the process
decision_process.narrative: Decision process partially visible from Next_Step__c but not confirmed on a call. The log (1 Jun 2026) references a planned 'CPO meeting' (Chief Procurement Officer, likely post-Raya in April 2026, status unclear as of 26 Jun) and work to engage 'CEO (Chin Keat)' and a 'board member,' suggesting a multi-level approval process typical of a healthcare group procurement decision: procurement function (Category Management head Mohd hafeiz) evaluates and recommends, CPO and CEO/board approve budget and vendor selection. The introductory meetings on 25 Feb 2026 involved 'proc and IT teams' (per Next_Step), indicating cross-functional stakeholder input (procurement + IT), but no recording exists to confirm the process. The 121-day Qualified tenure with no documented milestones (no demo, no RFP response, no shortlist, no commercial proposal) suggests the process has not formally started or is moving off-system. What remains unknown: approval thresholds, whether this is sole-sourced or competitive, whether a formal RFP/RFI will be issued, legal/InfoSec review requirements, and implementation-approval gates. Next move: confirm the decision process explicitly with the Decision Maker (Mohd hafeiz) in the next call—ask who sits on the evaluation committee, what the approval chain is (CPO → CEO → board?), what the timeline and gates are from evaluation to contract signature, and whether Zycus is sole-sourced or benchmarked against other vendors.

sources: Avoma: zero transcribed calls with criteria discussion
Next_Step__c 1 Jun: qualitative signals (Ariba dissatisfaction, IJN replication) but no formal criteria
No RFP/RFI or evaluation-scorecard fields populated
decision_criteria.narrative: Decision criteria not documented and not validated. Zero transcribed buyer calls and no RFP, RFI, or evaluation scorecard on record mean we do not know what KPJ will measure vendors against. Next_Step__c (1 Jun) implies functional fit (desire to 'replace Ariba' suggests dissatisfaction with incumbent capabilities) and a reference-case benchmark ('replicate IJN story' suggests a peer-healthcare success story is a decision input), but the actual evaluation dimensions (ease of use, integration with existing ERP/systems, mobile access, AI/automation appetite, implementation timeline, cost vs Ariba, vendor financial stability, local support in Malaysia) are unknown. The deal is in Qualified, so criteria should be emerging from discovery; the absence after 121 days signals incomplete qualification. The meeting on '25th with proc and IT teams' (likely 25 Feb or a future date, unclear from Next_Step) was intended to surface pain and presumably criteria, but no recording or summary exists. Next move: run a structured discovery workshop with procurement (Mohd hafeiz, Amir Farid) and IT (CIO Eric Kam Seng if involved in this opp) to document their evaluation framework, confirm whether this is sole-sourced or competitive (if Ariba replacement is pre-decided or if other vendors are in play), and align our demo/proof-of-value to their stated criteria before advancing to the relevant stakeholder.

Rep log 1 Jun 2026 notes ongoing outreach: regular catch-ups planned with board member (interested in Horizon, wants to replace Ariba), work to meet board member with CEO (Chin Keat) since 16 Mar, CPO meeting planned post-Raya (April), and meeting on 25th with proc/IT surfacing Ariba dissatisfaction and desire to replicate IJN story. Last Salesforce activity 13 Jun (13 days ago). No buyer-confirmed engagement, no advancement from Qualified, no demo/RFP/commercial milestone logged in 121 days. north_star_verdict.headline: Qualified-stage deal stalled 121 days with zero transcribed buyer calls and no EB or champion mapped. Rep outreach 25 days ago mentioned board interest and Ariba pain, but no confirmed buyer response or meeting booked since.

best_practice_check.summary: This deal is single-threaded (2 buyer contacts, no EB/Champion), stalled in Qualified for 121 days with zero transcribed buyer validation, and lacks a quantified business case or confirmed decision process to drive the Ariba displacement. Three high-leverage win-strategy levers: (1) Multi-thread aggressively to the Economic Buyer (CEO/CFO) and cultivate a champion (Head of Category Management or Head TMO) who can navigate the displacement internally and provide competitive intelligence. (2) Validate and quantify the Ariba pain on a recorded discovery call (cost, user adoption, compliance risk) and translate it into a具体 ROI case that the EB can authorize. (3) Activate the IJN reference case as the de-risking proof point: facilitate a customer call or site visit to show KPJ具体ly how a peer healthcare organization successfully replaced Ariba with Zycus, making the switch tangible and credible rather than abstract.

competitive_position.summary: SAP Ariba is the sole identified competitor, positioned as the incumbent being displaced due to rollout dissatisfaction (status: active incumbent, threat_level: high due to inertia and switching cost despite dissatisfaction). No external peer competitors (Coupa, GEP, Jaggaer) surfaced, likely reflecting incomplete discovery (zero transcribed calls) rather than confirmed sole-source status. The strongest current threat is status quo / do nothing: KPJ's dissatisfaction with Ariba has not translated into a formal evaluation or vendor selection process in 121 days, suggesting inertia or deprioritization. Ariba remains entrenched until a champion, quantified pain, and Economic Buyer authorization drive the displacement. Our wedge: healthcare-proven Ariba alternative (IJN reference case) with smoother deployment and local APAC support. Activation: quantify the Ariba pain, demo the具体 Zycus advantages on KPJ's pain points, and facilitate an IJN customer reference call to de-risk the switch before the CPO/CEO decision.

competitors.name: SAP Ariba
competitors.quote: unhappy with the way Ariba is rolled out; keen for options to replace Ariba
competitors.how_we_win: Position Zycus as the healthcare-tailored Ariba alternative with proven deployment success (IJN reference), local APAC support, and a quantified ROI case addressing KPJ's Ariba pain (rollout complexity, user adoption, cost). Bring the IJN story to life via customer call or site visit, and contrast our implementation approach (phased, hospital-by-hospital, with local support) against Ariba's failed rollout. Secure early champion buy-in (Mohd hafeiz or Head TMO) to navigate the displacement internally, and engage the Economic Buyer (CEO/CFO) with a C-level business case (cost savings, risk reduction, procurement efficiency) rather than a feature comparison.

competitors.name: Status Quo / Do Nothing
competitors.quote: 121 days in Qualified with no formal evaluation, no demo, no RFP, no commercial conversation logged; dissatisfaction stated but no action taken
competitors.source: Salesforce stage history and Next_Step__c activity log
competitors.how_we_win: Create urgency by quantifying the cost of inaction (ongoing Ariba pain: manual workarounds, compliance risk, lost procurement efficiency, high TCO). Tie the decision to a business event or forcing function (e.g. Ariba contract renewal date if available, upcoming procurement transformation initiative per Head TMO involvement, or budget cycle). Use the IJN success story to de-risk the change and show that switching is achievable. Engage the Economic Buyer (CEO/CFO) to frame this as a strategic procurement platform decision, not a tactical vendor swap, and align it to KPJ's digital transformation or operational excellence goals.

ai_positioning_strength.summary: AI positioning unknown. No AIS score, status, or rationale populated, and zero transcribed calls mean no buyer signal on AI appetite or interest in Zycus Merlin/agentic capabilities. Products include iSaaS (AI-enabled SaaS) in scope, but whether KPJ values AI-driven procurement automation, negotiation agents, or spend analytics is unvalidated. The desire to 'replicate IJN story' may imply interest in modern/innovative procurement tech (including AI if IJN's deployment featured it), but this is inference, not confirmed appetite.

items.quote: unhappy with the way Ariba is rolled out; wants to replicate IJN story
items.criterion: Ariba replacement with smoother rollout and better user adoption
Healthcare-specific procurement solution with peer reference case

customer_expectations_fit.summary: Fit expectations partially visible from Next_Step__c: KPJ wants a procurement solution that avoids Ariba's rollout pain and replicates a healthcare peer success (IJN). Product scope (iContract, iSource, eProcurement, eInvoicing, iSaaS) aligns with full-suite procurement platform replacement. However, fit is unvalidated due to zero transcribed discovery calls—具体 requirements (integration, mobile, AI/automation, multi-hospital deployment model, local support, cost vs Ariba) are unknown. No demo, no RFP response, no use-case validation logged in 121 days.

hard.ais_why:
• Across 332 chars of NextStep covering five dated entries from Mar to May 2026 ‚Äî including CEO meeting setup, CPO Raya meeting, and a reference customer story ‚Äî zero mention of AI, Merlin, agentic, automation or any AI capability. Product scope contains no AI SKUs (iContract; iSource; eProcurement; eInvoicing; iSaaS). On 12 Mar 2026: 'CPO meeting being setup after Raya - April Meting on 25th with proc and IT teams - unhappy with the way Ariba is rolled out. Wants to replicate IJN story.'
• The 30% confidence Mar update: 'Working to meet Dr. Siva (Board Member) along with Chin Keat.' KPJ wants to replicate the IJN story (Institut Jantung Negara, a Malaysian hospital reference) ‚Äî a deployment-pattern reference, not an AI capability reference. The deal is anchored in Ariba dissatisfaction and replication of a peer hospital, with no AI-specific evaluation criterion captured in any of the five rep notes.
• Confidence is Medium given the NextStep is short. With no AI products in scope and no AI keyword across the entire history, score 2 (AI Resistant) reflects a traditional e-procurement displacement deal where the buyer pattern is 'do what IJN did' rather than 'embrace AI'.

deltas.subject: No quantified business case or validated pain after 121 days and zero transcribed buyer calls. The Ariba dissatisfaction ('unhappy with rollout') is qualitative; without具体 cost/impact (annual Ariba spend, manual-workaround hours, compliance incidents, user adoption rate), the Economic Buyer has no ROI justification to authorize a $250k platform switch. Run a discovery workshop with procurement (Mohd hafeiz, Amir Farid) and IT (CIO if involved) to quantify the pain (what is broken, what is the annual cost/risk, who is affected) and build a savings model (cost reduction, efficiency gain, risk mitigation) anchored to KPJ's procurement volumes and Ariba TCO. Present the quantified case to the EB (CEO/CFO) as a strategic business decision, not a vendor swap.

deltas.subject: IJN reference case mentioned but not activated. Next_Step__c states KPJ 'wants to replicate IJN story,' signaling they need a healthcare peer proof point to de-risk the Ariba switch, but no customer call, site visit, or具体 case study has been delivered. Activate the IJN reference immediately: facilitate a customer-to-customer call between KPJ's procurement/IT leads and IJN's counterparts to discuss their Ariba displacement journey (pain, selection criteria, implementation experience, ROI achieved), or arrange a site visit if feasible. Make the IJN story tangible and credible—this is the single strongest tool to overcome inertia and switching-cost fear, but it only works if KPJ hears it directly from the peer, not from Zycus.

value.name: SAP Ariba
value.quote: unhappy with the way Ariba is rolled out; keen for options to replace Ariba
value.how_we_win: Position Zycus as the healthcare-tailored Ariba alternative with proven deployment success (IJN reference), local APAC support, and a quantified ROI case addressing KPJ's Ariba pain (rollout complexity, user adoption, cost). Bring the IJN story to life via customer call or site visit, and contrast our implementation approach (phased, hospital-by-hospital, with local support) against Ariba's failed rollout. Secure early champion buy-in (Mohd hafeiz or Head TMO) to navigate the displacement internally, and engage the Economic Buyer (CEO/CFO) with a C-level business case (cost savings, risk reduction, procurement efficiency) rather than a feature comparison.

value.name: Status Quo / Do Nothing
value.quote: 121 days in Qualified with no formal evaluation, no demo, no RFP, no commercial conversation logged; dissatisfaction stated but no action taken
value.source: Salesforce stage history and Next_Step__c activity log
value.how_we_win: Create urgency by quantifying the cost of inaction (ongoing Ariba pain: manual workarounds, compliance risk, lost procurement efficiency, high TCO). Tie the decision to a business event or forcing function (e.g. Ariba contract renewal date if available, upcoming procurement transformation initiative per Head TMO involvement, or budget cycle). Use the IJN success story to de-risk the change and show that switching is achievable. Engage the Economic Buyer (CEO/CFO) to frame this as a strategic procurement platform decision, not a tactical vendor swap, and align it to KPJ's digital transformation or operational excellence goals.
